Question: Q14
Verbatim: There are many unintentional errors that creep into work, or not even errors but places where we over-rationalize our own work and excuse (or don't even notice) deficiencies in our work. I'm always very appreciative when reviewers point out such issues in my manuscripts and I have the chance to consider and correct them. I try to pay that forward in my own reviewing work, where I try to take a respectful but critical eye to the papers that cross my desk, and don't just give them the 1 or 2 hour quick glance.   However, I feel that today's science doesn't reward correctness or thoroughness. We are all pushed to publish as many papers as possible, as quickly as possible, and preferably as flashy as possible (so they will be highly cited and put into well-regarded journals). This pressure not only encourages us to ignore (or take the time to correct) weaknesses in our own work, but also puts the average faculty member under tremendous time pressure, such that other service duties fundamental to science (rigorous and meaningful peer review, for example) are given short shrift.   Mistakes, disagreements, corrections, retractions, etc., are all fundamental to science and should be taken as such. Instead, we have poorly aligned incentives, and often adversarial relationships with the pubpeers and retractionwatches of the world. It's difficult to know how academic society should respond to these concerns, but in general in the few times I've been motivated to speak up regarding concerns about scientific correctness, I try to give the benefit of the doubt (I have been proven wrong by the authors of a "mistaken" paper before!). I think more rigor and more humility on the sides of both authors and reviewers is needed to keep scientific literature on a path to correctness.
CODE-h1: Reviewers point out error, authors glad to be notified about an error

Question: Q14
Verbatim: I'm working on ultrasensitive methods to detect minute concentrations of biomarkers (DNA or RNA for instance). In the literature, there is a lot of papers that claims a limit of detection in the sub-femtomolar or attomolar range. From my experience, such limits of detection seem very unlikely to be reached by such methods. My hypothesis is that the serial dilution of a synthetic target (used to evaluate the limit of detection) is biased by carry-over contamination and leads to an underestimation of the concentration. Although I have evidence that the method of performing the dilution is of extreme importance and can lead to differences by orders of magnitude in the actual concentration, I cannot formerly say that the experiments include such bias, precisely because the method of dilution is never reported. Although this seems anecdotal, I think it is important to report this problem. I will probably write a short scientific article about this issue that makes meaningless most of the reported limits of detection. I hope this information falls into the scope of your study.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: It has happened, for example, that a paper by a colleague contained an anomalous result, then drew a startling conclusion from it; that I attempted to reproduce his/her calculations and was unable to; that I ultimately succeeded in reverse-engineering their mistakes; that I contacted the author and showed my work; and that... nothing further happened. In another case, which happened while I was still a graduate student, I sent a Comment which could have been more diplomatically worded; got back a rather condescending Reply toComment which clearly was meant to encourage us to retract; and a few weeks later the authors published an erratum which not just corrected their numbers but overturned their conclusion. The published erratum did acknowledge me, but only after intervention (in pretty colorful language) by a third party who was in close touch with both of us.  On at least two occasions, scientists submitted papers over my explicit objection to what were in my opinion grave scientific errors; in one case (while I was still a postdoc), the paper was published in a no-name journal, never got cited, and I simply disowned it; in the second case (when I was a still fairly junior PI), it was submitted repeatedly to apex journals and only after being rejected repeatedly with withering referee comments, did my co-author relent and agreed to remove the error that led to the "spectacular" conclusion. The revised paper was duly published in a first-tier journal. I have never published another paper with this person, and whenever they asked for another collaboration, found excuses why I was too busy. Question: Q14

Papers are occasionally published in which the proposed mechanism to explain the outcome of a reaction is likely to be wrong. Usually, the problem is lack of supporting data for a reaction mechanism that is superficially plausible, but is probably incorrect. I have on occasion reviewed manuscripts in which the mechanistic interpretation of the data is clearly wrong and on those occasions I have rejected the manuscript or asked for major corrections to be made prior to publication. Editors are usually receptive to my comments.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Many colleagues these days simply don´t take their time for due dilligence, many papers I review it seems no senior author ever read them, certainly in most cases not the experimental/materials and methods part. There are often many mistakes in there. I feel often tempted to gloss over this part, too, as it is very time consuming, boring and we want to trust our junior colleagues that wrote this. Often also results, impact or relevance are exaggerated to the point where it becomes simply wrong, presumably because many authors feel the pressure to publish "high impact". In fact, I think the vast majority of papers contain significant errors, but mostly nobody cares anymore, because it´s kind of expected at this point. Another massive problem is that very often there will be peer-review networks, where authors choose "friendly reviewers", which of course is reciprocated for the next manuscript by the previous reviewer - now author. Another issue is that many PI´s will not even do the review, they give the manuscript to junior colleagues, maybe PhD students or junior postdocs to review. While these might try very hard to make a good review, they often lack experience and knowledge, and these PIs will typically not guide them sufficiently. Quite common practice unfortunately.  For many PIs, it seems the paper is the purpose of science, not the mere means by which (correct and trustworthy) scientific result are disseminated.

Question: Q14

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Academic scientific research is in my view highly sensationalist. The rewards for publishing "exciting" results are stronger than the rewards for publishing "rigorous" results. The consequence is that many high-profile studies make extraordinary claims but lack extraordinary evidence.  Sometimes the sensationalist "exciting" results that lack persuasive evidence turn out to be correct: either the researchers were lucky or the PI had good intuition. But sometimes, they turn out to be incorrect. Eventually people have trouble reproducing or building on the results. The trouble is that this self-correcting process can take far too long when the PI of the exciting result is a leader in the field and/or has a huge ego and fights fiercely to quash dissent.  The latter has been the case in my field, which only started about ten years ago. The claims are extraordinary, but most of the pioneering experiments come from one highly celebrated PI whose experiments lack the necessary controls to be persuasive. The field is now about ten years old and trouble is brewing: some of those who have attempted to reproduce experiments have failed, and theorists have struggled to come up with a theory that accurately predicts the experimental results. Our group currently has a paper under review which highlights the problem of designing rigorous control experiments in this field. We conducted an experiment that eliminated the problems and found that the conclusions of one pioneering paper could probably be explained by a trivial effect. During the review process 3 out of 4 reviewers acknowledged the problems we identified, but the fourth reviewer did not, and had a strangely intimate knowledge of the pioneering experiment whose results we challenged. (Hmm!) We will see if the paper gets published, but if it does not it will almost certainly be because ego is slowing down the scientific process.

Question: Q9_Auth_14_TEXT
Verbatim: I don't know if "error" is the right word, but, as I said earlier, I consider the more subtle issues of interpretation of data and finding better ways to analyze data in the context of new discoveries to be more important than pointing out the few "cheaters" or stupid mistakes that appear on twitter occasionally for folks to laugh at and feel good about themselves. The instance I am thinking of is data I collected and interpreted in graduate school that I now suspect has a different meaning than I assigned it then. I, and the field, know much more and looking back it is likely a different case than was assigned in the paper. I am open about this and not trying to hide something (not that many people would care, I think - it isn't a huge field with dramatic consequences), but the current system makes errors like this very hard to address. I don't consider this "my" work since I was a PhD student, so it seems like an imposition to "refute" my former advisors work. Also, all new data would need to be collected and the synthesis is not something my lab does, and it would not be an "interesting" publication, so, if the conclusions to be drawn were, as I suspect, different than previously published, it would be an expensive, time-consuming, low-impact way to create some ill-will while ultimately changing very little.   The way to actually change this is in the way we are credited for data. If I had an annotated dataset in a searchable database that was separate from my analysis, then I could now reanalyze that data as part of a larger study that involved refining the model I had used (and also modifying the (currently non-existent) raw data check to test for the measurement errors that caused the analysis error in the first place). In this scenario, everyone wins because everyone with data that had the errors gets cited and they get their data reanalyzed in a new paper.

There are often many mistakes in there. I feel often tempted to gloss over this part, too, as it is very time consuming, boring and we want to trust our junior colleagues that wrote this. Often also results, impact or relevance are exaggerated to the point where it becomes simply wrong, presumably because many authors feel the pressure to publish "high impact". In fact, I think the vast majority of papers contain significant errors, but mostly nobody cares anymore, because it´s kind of expected at this point. Another massive problem is that very often there will be peer-review networks, where authors choose "friendly reviewers", which of course is reciprocated for the next manuscript by the previous reviewer - now author. Another issue is that many PI´s will not even do the review, they give the manuscript to junior colleagues, maybe PhD students or junior postdocs to review. While these might try very hard to make a good review, they often lack experience and knowledge, and these PIs will typically not guide them sufficiently. Quite common practice unfortunately.  For many PIs, it seems the paper is the purpose of science, not the mere means by which (correct and trustworthy) scientific result are disseminated.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Sometimes, scientific errors are holes that can be patched by the authors with a bit more effort. However, often when the hole is found and admitted to, competing scientists often dominant in the prevailing  pecking order in the research community rush in to fix the hole and use it as an opportunity to claim credit.   I happen to hold a strong opinion that "credit" is mostly illusory as  anyone among a whole community of researchers could potentially have made any given contribution no matter how much "genius"it required.   However (a) mine is a heterodox opinion, (b) pecking orders are unfortunately very real although I do not believe they are an immutable law of sociology as we are led to believe and (c) opportunities to function with the required degree of freedom as a scientist are - currently - invariably tied to these attributions of credit.   So when credit it usurped, it in fact has seriously deleterious consequences for the fabric of the research community overall.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: I would first want to encourage you on the choice of this project. in research, we encountered a highly influential field (attracting 100s of million USD of research funding in the US only over the last decade) where nearly all previous theoretical and experimental work was faulty; some of the errors were obvious and even infuriating. moreover, they were repeatedly done by some of the most famous scientists in my community and published in a decade-long series of papers in journals of the highest profile.  while the series of comments we wrote on these papers had some effect, our experience is that the most important ingredients were missing. those are   1. echoing of our criticism by leading researchers - while countless senior scientists in our community expressed full support in our criticism in private conversations, none had expressed it in public. some used the excuse of "I cannot run after every single error by others", "these scientists are too big to "fall", or simply hinted that they must maintain good relations with those influential researchers that published the faulty results.  2. cooperation from editors - while we had one good experience with one Science editor, our experience with other editors of that journal, as well as editors in other Nature group and ACS journals was that they want to avoid the scandal, or avoid a conflict with senior scientists who published faulty results. one even wrote that "our journal does not publish criticism over earlier work, but instead focuses on high end original research"... in other words, our feeling is that the editors are more committed to the financial success and reputation of their journal (not to mention their professional relations with those shady researchers) rather than to truth... In my experience, it is frustrating that even as a peer reviewer I probably could have found more errors that could have been corrected beforehand. - The main obstacle to improve the self-correcting ability of science is that it requires a change in intellectual culture, away from gamification (stats, prestige, individual truths, citation cliques) and toward extreme scrutiny of one own's work before publication or preprint uploading.

Question: Q9_Auth_14_TEXT
Verbatim: I don't know if "error" is the right word, but, as I said earlier, I consider the more subtle issues of interpretation of data and finding better ways to analyze data in the context of new discoveries to be more important than pointing out the few "cheaters" or stupid mistakes that appear on twitter occasionally for folks to laugh at and feel good about themselves. The instance I am thinking of is data I collected and interpreted in graduate school that I now suspect has a different meaning than I assigned it then. I, and the field, know much more and looking back it is likely a different case than was assigned in the paper. I am open about this and not trying to hide something (not that many people would care, I think - it isn't a huge field with dramatic consequences), but the current system makes errors like this very hard to address. I don't consider this "my" work since I was a PhD student, so it seems like an imposition to "refute" my former advisors work. Also, all new data would need to be collected and the synthesis is not something my lab does, and it would not be an "interesting" publication, so, if the conclusions to be drawn were, as I suspect, different than previously published, it would be an expensive, time-consuming, low-impact way to create some ill-will while ultimately changing very little.   The way to actually change this is in the way we are credited for data. If I had an annotated dataset in a searchable database that was separate from my analysis, then I could now reanalyze that data as part of a larger study that involved refining the model I had used (and also modifying the (currently non-existent) raw data check to test for the measurement errors that caused the analysis error in the first place). In this scenario, everyone wins because everyone with data that had the errors gets cited and they get their data reanalyzed in a new paper.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Just a comment, I think in the future we need a dramatic reshaping of our current platforms for sharing research. The research community has dramatically expanded in recent years, which is great, but it has also led to much larger amounts of low quality / inaccurate research being published, which greatly dilutes the scientific literature – which is already impossible to keep up with. Future AI models which will likely be used to summarise the literature for human researchers to digest will inevitably be susceptible to such low-quality published results (though I guess they might end up with a 'trustworthy' metric built-in to ignore results from journals/authors that have been identified as poor quality).  I think the current 'static' approach of publishing a fixed, unchanging journal article will likely need to change in the future, possibly adopting something more similar to the git version control for software packages. This would allow the easy rectification of scientific errors in-place (rather than corrigenda which are a pain for authors to do, and also come with no benefit), and facilitate greater community discussion to identify errors and contribute to solving them.   In summary, I think ERC money would be well-spent on trying to develop a new open-source platform for sharing science, which can resolve these issues but also allow appropriate recognition of researchers' work (for obtaining academic positions).  I'm a final year PhD student who has published 25 scientific articles, in journals including Nature Photonics, Nature Communications and Physical Review Letters.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: I know few authors publish papers with partial results, just to boost their claims. For example, if a reaction shows maximum results with 1 mg of catalyst, but very poor results with say 10 or 25 mg of catalyst, the latter is suppressed. As it has become a bit widespread problem, I indicated this in my review published 2 years back and made the readers aware of this issue. Instead of understanding the real issue, defending authors get actually feel offended. Once, I was persuaded through my senior colleague to withdraw my comment manuscript, but I did not budge.   Sometime I also feel that, who am I, to correct these deliberate defaulters ?   Sometime back I got a manuscript from very high impact (~40) journal. Even while going through the manuscript, I realized that it was such a poor manuscript, but still sent out for review by Editor. I promptly made all scientific comments and recommend to reject the manuscript. This high impact journal is happy to claim that they reject more than 90 % of submitted manuscripts. I also wrote in the confidential column that this manuscript should have been rejected even without review. Within 2-3 weeks, the same rejected manuscript came to me for review from a sister journal of the same publishing group; this also happens to be the next high impact journal. Even a cursory look indicated that the authors never answered any of my questions/comments/suggestions. I indeed informed the Editor that it was already rejected from your sister journal and none of my comments were answered. My point is, even Editors decision is increasingly becomeing subjective. At one occasion I encountered a published paper where the authors quoted one of my papers stating exactly the opposite of what was the conclusion of that paper.  At another occasion I encountered a published paper, with authors from elsewhere, which almost verbatim copied the text from one of my papers.   At one or two occasions I received mail from somebody who pointed out some misprint in one of my papers, which I subsequently corrected in an erratum or in a follow-up paper.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: During my review process, I found that the authors used almost the same sentences as those in my papers.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: I found a case of plagiarism in a review article, contacted the editor who replied that they take the issue seriously. But a while after the reply, the review article was still available.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: In one instance, I read a paper in which a majority of the introduction was plagiarized from work I had previously published (I would otherwise have been unlikely to realize the plagiarism). I emailed the editor in chief of they journal, but they never responded or corrected the offending article. It was disappointing; while it has no impact on science, I strive to achieve a high degree of ethics in the work that I do, and I demand the same from others because without ethics, science simply cannot function.   I will say, though, that I do have some sympathy for the authors of the offending work, as they were clearly not native English speakers, and I do suspect some differences in cultural norms led to the authors not fully appreciating the ethical norms they were crossing. In this instance, I would certainly support informing the authors in a quiet manner and allowing them to revise the paper without fanfare. My interest is more in correcting the record, rather than punishing authors for transgressions (noting that fabrication of data is a whole different ballgame).

Question: Q14
Verbatim: In my field - synthetic organic chemistry - obvious errors are fairly rare in well established high quality international journals because a strong evidence base is required in order to publish in these journals and compound characterisation techniques are well developed and usually lead to unambiguous structural assignments. When errors do arise, these are normally associated with mechanistic interpretation of the data, which is a much more contestable area than structural assignment. Papers are occasionally published in which the proposed mechanism to explain the outcome of a reaction is likely to be wrong. Usually, the problem is lack of supporting data for a reaction mechanism that is superficially plausible, but is probably incorrect. I have on occasion reviewed manuscripts in which the mechanistic interpretation of the data is clearly wrong and on those occasions I have rejected the manuscript or asked for major corrections to be made prior to publication. Editors are usually receptive to my comments.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Good Experience: - by sharing concerns with the community you realise that you are not the only one who thinks the same way, people/journals are supportive of such "correct science" mentality and approach BUT unfortunately only few scientists are ready to undertake studies to debunk ambiguous data/papers/conclusions. People prefer to focus on "new stuff" rather than debunking "old stuff"...  Bad experience:  - passive aggressive, non-requested emails from authors of past papers/data "debunked", despite wording the new research paper very carefully to sound supportive/constructive and not dismissive of past work. - some funders keep supporting individuals consistently (not necessarily intentionally) reporting questionable data in well regarded journals, despite the concerns being raised in other peer-reviewed articles. There are still "hotspots" where status/knowledge of an individual or institution still prevails on rigor. Having studied/worked in some fortunate and less fortunate institutions, I can see this still happens regularly.

Question: Q14

Question: Q14

Question: Q14
Verbatim: I am presently invovled in attempts to comment on papers promoting cold fusion nonsense. Most journals appear very reluctant to admit even a comment on those papers, much less retract them. Science is in hock to the publication industry and those people care nothing about science. The malaise has spread to learned society journals.

Question: Q14

Question: Q14
Verbatim: I think I disagree with the thesis that science fails to correct itself. Possibly not on the timescale that we would like, but important faulty papers will fall through as people will try to use the science. Those not discovered, maybe it does not matter, because the paper is not important enough. In any case, any natural system will contain errors, only in theory errors may not exist.  Could we distinguish three types of errors, as a suggestion? 1) Honest interpretation errors, 2) political errors, and 3) commercial errors, although a single error may simultaneously fall within multiple categories.  1) We're all fallible and it happens that we overlook things, unfortunate but it is part of the deal  2) For example careerists that need to publish high impact and make up stories, so here there is a clear intent to produce faulty science (or to be negligent at the least) for their own gain (these are the ones we tend to see, because they fall hard if discovered and they cause the largest damage to the respect for science of the general public)  3) It bothers me that many new journals (open access...???) demand very short review times and basically depend on high article throughput. They publish whatever, even if the review is negative (from my own experience). The open access model is more prone to this type of commercial push it through without proper review error than the older journals, because more papers = more profit. This fashion of quick review should be really ditched! Science should stay away from HOT, FAST and fashionable; that's a breeding ground of errors (categories 1, 2, and 3!!). Papers can be published right away on a preprint server, which should SUPPORT solid and proper review for the final official paper as a serious added value for a journal.
CODE-h1: Time consuming to correct, lack of time

Question: Q14
Verbatim: To me the major problem we are facing now is the exploding number of so called "predatory journals" aking to be paid to provide open access, and claim to be peer-reviewed. These journals "buy" their reviewers by offering them free publication in their next paper. They receive manuscripts, send the manuscript to their reviewers, ask a report in one week and, whatever the reports say, the manuscript will be published... This systems attracts potential reviewers because they will be waived of publication fee and potential authors because of an ultra fast publication scheme and open access option. I have several colleagues who reviewed for such journals. They all told me the same things. 1) yhe manuscript(s) they reviewed was (were) of poor quality; 2) they had pressure from the editors to review in an utra-short time (typically one week); 3) whatever my colleagues wrote in their reports (constructive criticisms, request for additional experiment, etc.), the manuscript was very quickly accepted, without going back to the reviewers and many time with no modification. This system is a vicious circle that propagates scientific errors in the published works in dramatic and exponential manner. Nowadays, I find that most manuscripts in preprint servers (chemRxiv, bioRxiv) are of much better scientific quality (no or few errors) than all these predatory journals pretending to be peer-reviewed. Many of these journals are proudly advertising on their average time beteen submission and acceptance/publication that can be as short as 7 days, with impact factors as high as 5, 7 or even more! It is just impossible to have a fair and useful peer-review process (reading, checking, reporting on error by the reviewers, then obtaining additional data, correction and improving the revised manuscript by the authors) in such a short time.
CODE-h1: Time consuming to correct, lack of time

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Thank you for your important work! Scientific errors in our field become in many different flavors. Some are small mistakes in e.g. calibration values which keep repeating and rarely affect the conclusions of the individual papers, but when different studies are compared, they can lead to confusion. Then there are "public secrets" that some of the studies were maybe not so well performed and the results are overinterpreted, but no one does anything for the matter. I have found it useful to share my mistakes with students, since everyone does mistakes. I explain that are formal procedures to follow to make an erratum. What might be discouraging for people to submit the erratum or retract the paper is that it might be connected in people's mind to intentional manipulation of data or other fraud. No one wants to have that kind of reputation. However, I know a group which withdraw a paper from a prestigious journal since they find out in a later study that the calibration of their instrument was not correctly performed. In my opinion this just increased the respect to the group.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: As a general comment, an accelerated rate of publishing imposed by the community and funding entities leads to increased errors in scientific papers. Today scientists have no time to re-think the data and results published by other groups. The pressure on publishing limits the time for reading and thus correcting errors.  In my research field, I see that publications containing questionable or unreproducible results will shortly vanish, and no one will cite or use these works for any good in the next 10 or 20 years. I do hope that science will self-correct in long term.  Thanks for rising this aspect.

Apparently some journals are not interested in correcting errors.  2) We observed an error in a publication, and wrote to the author (name-by name). In his further work, he corrected the error, but did not acknowledge us at all.  3) A colleague observed an strong error in a publication of another colleague. The publication was in a high-ranking journal (Nature).Some time later, it became obvious that the other colleague had done multiple scientific frauds, e.g. he wrote papers on experimental results, but he had never carried out any experiments. Question: Q14
Verbatim: As I mentioned above, my worst experiences were as a referee whose knowledge is not taken into account by the editor. In a case particularly worth mentionning, a crystallographic work had been submitted to "[ANONYMISED]", a journal were there is no crystallographer in the editorial board. The authors had mistaken substrate X-ray reflections for those of their objects, and wrongly interpreted them in terms of a metastable phase of silicon that would have wonderful electronic properties. For their defense, the substrate reflections are normally forbidden but do occur in certain conditions. The particular editor in charge of the paper was obviously seduced by the fact of publishing a new metastable phase of silicon. The two other referees had obviously no competence in crystallography. Here is the reference of the published paper, which I recommended not to publish at all: [ANONYMISED]
CODE-h1: Reviewers point out error, reviewers' comment ignored

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Editors are not always willing to correct errors in papers in their journals. It has happened regularly that I flagged issues with data processing or interpretation with editors during the peer-review process, which were then ignored and led to manuscripts being published of low scientific quality. I know second-hand of another case of authors publishing a long string of papers full of serious errors in data interpretation. My colleague, who was invited to review a new paper by these authors, made a well-argued case that new work by these authors should be more strongly scrutinized and past papers should really be retracted. His comments were ignored, and the new paper was published without many changes.  In general, the impression of peer-review in my specific field is not entirely positive. Frequently, fellow reviewers on manuscripts that I peer-review are not nearly critical enough and let a lot of errors pass. This makes me worry that many mistakes propagate on manuscripts that do not have a sufficiently critical reviewer.
CODE-h1: Reviewers point out error, reviewers' comment ignored

Question: Q14
Verbatim: I know few authors publish papers with partial results, just to boost their claims. For example, if a reaction shows maximum results with 1 mg of catalyst, but very poor results with say 10 or 25 mg of catalyst, the latter is suppressed. When we try to replicate results of that authors, it wont fit their claims of normalized results per gram of catalyst. As it has become a bit widespread problem, I indicated this in my review published 2 years back and made the readers aware of this issue.   Further, when you submit a comment manuscript, usually the reply from the defending authors comes in a real bad language. Instead of understanding the real issue, defending authors get actually feel offended. Once, I was persuaded through my senior colleague to withdraw my comment manuscript, but I did not budge.   Sometime I also feel that, who am I, to correct these deliberate defaulters ?   Sometime back I got a manuscript from very high impact (~40) journal. Even while going through the manuscript, I realized that it was such a poor manuscript, but still sent out for review by Editor. I promptly made all scientific comments and recommend to reject the manuscript. This high impact journal is happy to claim that they reject more than 90 % of submitted manuscripts. I also wrote in the confidential column that this manuscript should have been rejected even without review. Within 2-3 weeks, the same rejected manuscript came to me for review from a sister journal of the same publishing group; this also happens to be the next high impact journal. Even a cursory look indicated that the authors never answered any of my questions/comments/suggestions. I indeed informed the Editor that it was already rejected from your sister journal and none of my comments were answered. My point is, even Editors decision is increasingly becomeing subjective. Some Editors are also biased towards certain countries, either blindly promote or blindly reject the articles.
CODE-h1: Reviewers point out error, reviewers' comment ignored

Question: Q3_notAuth_10_TEXT
Verbatim: In most cases the only time I "take action" is when reviewing a paper, I will try to point out any vague and unsupported conclusions and ways that the conclusions could be verified in my mind. In the current publishing environment, I am also mindful of the fact that authors are dependent on publications to be able to work (research universities largely treat the research portion as a source of money and the researchers as tenants) and the fact that the manuscript I am reviewing will end up in the literature somewhere no matter what due to the number of places where one can publish. With these in mind, I consider it my "job" (unpaid, and, ironically, usually to put research behind a paywall), to reason with the authors and ask for logical changes that should appeal to them as scientists. When asking for new experiments, I think it is important to make them within the means of the authors, doable on a reasonable timeline, and essential to a conclusion presented in the paper. Unless it affects the entire paper, I will suggest simply removing the conclusion I feel is untenable.   I will also point out issues with data to my students and colleagues when it is important to collaborations or interpretation of our results. I will also sometimes discuss it directly with the authors at a conference is I know them well enough for it to not be confrontational. The main problem is that in most fields including mine, there is not enough data in the manuscript to know for certain which errors are being made. In fact, with current analysis methods this is not likely possible without significant time and money put into the data collection, analysis, storage, and organization methods, but the resources for these types of undertakings are not available in most areas of science, which is, perhaps, the root of the problem.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: There are many unintentional errors that creep into work, or not even errors but places where we over-rationalize our own work and excuse (or don't even notice) deficiencies in our work. I'm always very appreciative when reviewers point out such issues in my manuscripts and I have the chance to consider and correct them. I try to pay that forward in my own reviewing work, where I try to take a respectful but critical eye to the papers that cross my desk, and don't just give them the 1 or 2 hour quick glance.   However, I feel that today's science doesn't reward correctness or thoroughness. We are all pushed to publish as many papers as possible, as quickly as possible, and preferably as flashy as possible (so they will be highly cited and put into well-regarded journals). This pressure not only encourages us to ignore (or take the time to correct) weaknesses in our own work, but also puts the average faculty member under tremendous time pressure, such that other service duties fundamental to science (rigorous and meaningful peer review, for example) are given short shrift.   Mistakes, disagreements, corrections, retractions, etc., are all fundamental to science and should be taken as such. Instead, we have poorly aligned incentives, and often adversarial relationships with the pubpeers and retractionwatches of the world. It's difficult to know how academic society should respond to these concerns, but in general in the few times I've been motivated to speak up regarding concerns about scientific correctness, I try to give the benefit of the doubt (I have been proven wrong by the authors of a "mistaken" paper before!). I think more rigor and more humility on the sides of both authors and reviewers is needed to keep scientific literature on a path to correctness.

Careful selection of Reviewers is needed as well as perhaps many more than the usual number due to the complexity of the subject material. This fashion of quick review should be really ditched! Science should stay away from HOT, FAST and fashionable; that's a breeding ground of errors (categories 1, 2, and 3!!). Question: Q14
Verbatim: To me the major problem we are facing now is the exploding number of so called "predatory journals" aking to be paid to provide open access, and claim to be peer-reviewed. These journals "buy" their reviewers by offering them free publication in their next paper. They receive manuscripts, send the manuscript to their reviewers, ask a report in one week and, whatever the reports say, the manuscript will be published... This systems attracts potential reviewers because they will be waived of publication fee and potential authors because of an ultra fast publication scheme and open access option. I have several colleagues who reviewed for such journals. They all told me the same things. 1) yhe manuscript(s) they reviewed was (were) of poor quality; 2) they had pressure from the editors to review in an utra-short time (typically one week); 3) whatever my colleagues wrote in their reports (constructive criticisms, request for additional experiment, etc.), the manuscript was very quickly accepted, without going back to the reviewers and many time with no modification. This system is a vicious circle that propagates scientific errors in the published works in dramatic and exponential manner. Nowadays, I find that most manuscripts in preprint servers (chemRxiv, bioRxiv) are of much better scientific quality (no or few errors) than all these predatory journals pretending to be peer-reviewed. Many of these journals are proudly advertising on their average time beteen submission and acceptance/publication that can be as short as 7 days, with impact factors as high as 5, 7 or even more! It is just impossible to have a fair and useful peer-review process (reading, checking, reporting on error by the reviewers, then obtaining additional data, correction and improving the revised manuscript by the authors) in such a short time.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Editors are not always willing to correct errors in papers in their journals. It has happened regularly that I flagged issues with data processing or interpretation with editors during the peer-review process, which were then ignored and led to manuscripts being published of low scientific quality. I know second-hand of another case of authors publishing a long string of papers full of serious errors in data interpretation. My colleague, who was invited to review a new paper by these authors, made a well-argued case that new work by these authors should be more strongly scrutinized and past papers should really be retracted. His comments were ignored, and the new paper was published without many changes.  In general, the impression of peer-review in my specific field is not entirely positive. Frequently, fellow reviewers on manuscripts that I peer-review are not nearly critical enough and let a lot of errors pass. This makes me worry that many mistakes propagate on manuscripts that do not have a sufficiently critical reviewer.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Sometimes, scientific errors are holes that can be patched by the authors with a bit more effort. However, often when the hole is found and admitted to, competing scientists often dominant in the prevailing  pecking order in the research community rush in to fix the hole and use it as an opportunity to claim credit.   I happen to hold a strong opinion that "credit" is mostly illusory as  anyone among a whole community of researchers could potentially have made any given contribution no matter how much "genius"it required.   However (a) mine is a heterodox opinion, (b) pecking orders are unfortunately very real although I do not believe they are an immutable law of sociology as we are led to believe and (c) opportunities to function with the required degree of freedom as a scientist are - currently - invariably tied to these attributions of credit.   So when credit it usurped, it in fact has seriously deleterious consequences for the fabric of the research community overall.
CODE-h1: Concerns about the common good, the community, the consequences of propagation

Verbatim: Few comments: there are different types of errors, larger and bigger probably very few papers are fully error-free. Question: Q4_notAuth_8_TEXT
Verbatim: I think it is naive to expect all scientists to react to all published papers. Indeed, I think even the way the question was posed here shows a naive way of how science progresses (what is a "scientific error"? Something I am thoroughly convinced is wrong, something the current consensus opinion deem is wrong, or something that goes against a consensus that has been established for decades?). A consensus is formed by giving more evidence for a particular hypothesis, rather than criticising each and every paper published in the literature that goes against my (or someone else's opinion). In fact, I cannot see any other way to progress, given that often (some) researchers are wrong, and sometimes consensus are wrong. This is not to say that "letters to the editor" and "replies" are not a useful format of scientific discourse, but they one of several options, and I would say scientists choose one or the other depending upon the current state of the field.

In fact, I cannot see any other way to progress, given that often (some) researchers are wrong, and sometimes consensus are wrong. This is not to say that "letters to the editor" and "replies" are not a useful format of scientific discourse, but they one of several options, and I would say scientists choose one or the other depending upon the current state of the field.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: To add a brief comment:  I rarely spotted an error that would completely bring down the paper. I often find that the results in a paper are interpreted in the wrong terms, but I would say this is quite common. We once published a paper to refute a particularly wrong paper, which sparked discussion at conferences and subsequent publications.   The majority of cases I am aware of consist of miscalculated experimental quantities that bring to an exaggerated claim. For example, is the effect 5% or 50%? There's the ubiquitous problem of background subtraction which can change things dramatically.  The general feeling is that if the paper is not particularly impactful or brings incremental information in the field, the need for correction is ignored. Question: Q14
Verbatim: I know few authors publish papers with partial results, just to boost their claims. For example, if a reaction shows maximum results with 1 mg of catalyst, but very poor results with say 10 or 25 mg of catalyst, the latter is suppressed. When we try to replicate results of that authors, it wont fit their claims of normalized results per gram of catalyst. As it has become a bit widespread problem, I indicated this in my review published 2 years back and made the readers aware of this issue.   Further, when you submit a comment manuscript, usually the reply from the defending authors comes in a real bad language. Instead of understanding the real issue, defending authors get actually feel offended. Once, I was persuaded through my senior colleague to withdraw my comment manuscript, but I did not budge.   Sometime I also feel that, who am I, to correct these deliberate defaulters ?   Sometime back I got a manuscript from very high impact (~40) journal. Even while going through the manuscript, I realized that it was such a poor manuscript, but still sent out for review by Editor. I promptly made all scientific comments and recommend to reject the manuscript. This high impact journal is happy to claim that they reject more than 90 % of submitted manuscripts. I also wrote in the confidential column that this manuscript should have been rejected even without review. Within 2-3 weeks, the same rejected manuscript came to me for review from a sister journal of the same publishing group; this also happens to be the next high impact journal. Even a cursory look indicated that the authors never answered any of my questions/comments/suggestions. I indeed informed the Editor that it was already rejected from your sister journal and none of my comments were answered. My point is, even Editors decision is increasingly becomeing subjective. Some Editors are also biased towards certain countries, either blindly promote or blindly reject the articles.
CODE-h1: Editors are not qualified, are wrong

Question: Q2_notAuth_15_TEXT
Verbatim: I was a reviewer of the paper in question.  I had pointed out the error in my initial review, then reviewed a revision and pointed out that the error still existed.   I did not hear back from the editor and to my surprise the paper appeared in final form in the journal.  I contacted the editor and he said that he had previously agreed with the authors' rebuttal to my point, without contacting me.  From my correspondence, he realized that he was wrong and offered to contact the authors.  I responded that this was unproductive at this point.  I pointed out the error in a subsequent paper of mine.  To me, this illustrates the fact that the editor was insufficiently knowledgeable concerning the subject matter.  I have seen similar problems with other published papers that contain errors (in my experience, most often in "open access" journals).  This illustrates the problem of both incompetent reviewers and editors.
CODE-h1: Editors are not qualified, are wrong

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Editors are not always willing to correct errors in papers in their journals. It has happened regularly that I flagged issues with data processing or interpretation with editors during the peer-review process, which were then ignored and led to manuscripts being published of low scientific quality. I know second-hand of another case of authors publishing a long string of papers full of serious errors in data interpretation. My colleague, who was invited to review a new paper by these authors, made a well-argued case that new work by these authors should be more strongly scrutinized and past papers should really be retracted. His comments were ignored, and the new paper was published without many changes.  In general, the impression of peer-review in my specific field is not entirely positive. Frequently, fellow reviewers on manuscripts that I peer-review are not nearly critical enough and let a lot of errors pass. This makes me worry that many mistakes propagate on manuscripts that do not have a sufficiently critical reviewer.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: I found an error in the article that was published in one of the flagship chemical journal. I was trying to repeat the published procedure, but I failed. I asked my colleagues to repeat the procedure, but they also failed. The reaction mechanism given in the article was in contradiction to the general knowledge. In addition, the paper describing this reaction was already published in the early 80s, but, of course, it was not cited. Nevertheless, we have performed a full analysis of the products and reaction mechanism. We decided to first write a letter to the corresponding author. One of them was a very recognizable chemist from China (H-index > 80). Despite several attempts of contact, we did not get any answer. We have decided to contact Editor. We have sent all the data and evidence pointing out that the reaction was already studied but the publication was not even cited. The Editor first take serious our objections, he contacted the authors, but they laconically replayed that everything is correct, completely avoiding the discussion. The Editor only forwarded their answer to us and did not take any further action. Discouraged by the Editor's lack of willingness to solve this problem, we took no further steps...

Question: Q14
Verbatim: I once reviewed a paper that contained a completely incorrect enzymatic mechanism. The authors already published a previous article with the same error in that same journal.  I recommended a rejection of the paper I reviewed, but also mentioned to the editor that the already published paper contained the same fundamental error and should in my opinion be retracted. I am considering writing a formal letter to the editor about it, but have not been able to get around to it yet. This bothers me, since in my opinion my original flagging to the editor of the second paper should have lead to action against the previous paper as well, since the error in the paper was very serious and fundamental, as well as clearly evident.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: I would first want to encourage you on the choice of this project. in research, we encountered a highly influential field (attracting 100s of million USD of research funding in the US only over the last decade) where nearly all previous theoretical and experimental work was faulty; some of the errors were obvious and even infuriating. moreover, they were repeatedly done by some of the most famous scientists in my community and published in a decade-long series of papers in journals of the highest profile.  while the series of comments we wrote on these papers had some effect, our experience is that the most important ingredients were missing. those are   1. echoing of our criticism by leading researchers - while countless senior scientists in our community expressed full support in our criticism in private conversations, none had expressed it in public. some used the excuse of "I cannot run after every single error by others", "these scientists are too big to "fall", or simply hinted that they must maintain good relations with those influential researchers that published the faulty results.  2. cooperation from editors - while we had one good experience with one Science editor, our experience with other editors of that journal, as well as editors in other Nature group and ACS journals was that they want to avoid the scandal, or avoid a conflict with senior scientists who published faulty results. one even wrote that "our journal does not publish criticism over earlier work, but instead focuses on high end original research"... in other words, our feeling is that the editors are more committed to the financial success and reputation of their journal (not to mention their professional relations with those shady researchers) rather than to truth...  It is our impression that without these ingredients, the pace of self-correction in science is significantly slowed down, or even abolished altogether.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Overall, we see less corrections published than was previously the case 10 or 20 years ago. When I recently submitted a correction, the reviewers suggested that I submit the revised work as an original contribution and my attempts to include the original publication in the title or abstract were also not accepted. Over my 40 years of reviewing 40+ papers each year, the most negative experience has been when I spent some serious time and effort helping to improve a manuscript and the editor allowed it to be published without revision! Question: Q14

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Some questions are not precisely formulated. One example:  will SUBMITTION of comment correct the science?   No, it is only publication of comment which might correct the error. Unfortunately 99% of submitted comments are not published. Also when you write "error", it is assumed by the meaning as unintentional.   In recent  years I faced hundreds of papers with outrageously fabricated data. These are not errors but intentional fraud. In some cases I managed to force retractions, but in most cases journals and editors refuse to do anything, do not reply at all in many cases, issue corrections replacing several fake figures. I am also aware of cases when fake figure was silently replaced in the published paper without citing the problem or issuing correction, simply one file replaced by another.  If editors help to authors to hide their fakes, the whole system is doomed.    I am right emotional now as one of my best papers was not send for review citing lack of novelty. As an evidence, the journal  cited  a paper which   has several comments on pubpeer exposing several fake figures, also exposed several years ago.  Not even statement of concern was issues by IF =10 journal. I contacted Editor in Chief to ask why they took no actions and got no reply at all.  Do you name it "error"? I name it catastrophe for the whole publishing system. For one occasion when editors took my report seriously and retracted paper with fabricated data I have tens examples of complete ignorance.  Consider interviewing Elisabeth Bik for statistics, only about 10% of reports finish with some action. I confirm it.

In another case where I did not know the authors and where we had been working on the same system, it was much easier to publish a firm refutation of their work (simultaneously done by others also). That case was also revealing because the reviewers of the erroneous paper had queried the results (the reviews were included in the publication), but the authors had managed to get away with a half-hearted response (again the authors had not acted fraudulently, but were sure of their results). I my view the editor should have acted here.  3. I recently was asked to review a paper which was a direct refutation of work by a group that I know of (but no collaborative contact). In this case I felt that the refuting paper was too direct and certain in its criticism because the experiments conducted were not identical to those in the original paper, and noted my concerns to the editor - I felt that the editor should have toned down the refuting paper with caveats, although some were introduced following my review.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: There are many unintentional errors that creep into work, or not even errors but places where we over-rationalize our own work and excuse (or don't even notice) deficiencies in our work. I'm always very appreciative when reviewers point out such issues in my manuscripts and I have the chance to consider and correct them. I try to pay that forward in my own reviewing work, where I try to take a respectful but critical eye to the papers that cross my desk, and don't just give them the 1 or 2 hour quick glance.   However, I feel that today's science doesn't reward correctness or thoroughness. We are all pushed to publish as many papers as possible, as quickly as possible, and preferably as flashy as possible (so they will be highly cited and put into well-regarded journals). This pressure not only encourages us to ignore (or take the time to correct) weaknesses in our own work, but also puts the average faculty member under tremendous time pressure, such that other service duties fundamental to science (rigorous and meaningful peer review, for example) are given short shrift.   Mistakes, disagreements, corrections, retractions, etc., are all fundamental to science and should be taken as such. Instead, we have poorly aligned incentives, and often adversarial relationships with the pubpeers and retractionwatches of the world. It's difficult to know how academic society should respond to these concerns, but in general in the few times I've been motivated to speak up regarding concerns about scientific correctness, I try to give the benefit of the doubt (I have been proven wrong by the authors of a "mistaken" paper before!). I think more rigor and more humility on the sides of both authors and reviewers is needed to keep scientific literature on a path to correctness.
CODE-h1: Errors are part of the scientific process

Question: Q14
Verbatim: I would first want to encourage you on the choice of this project. in research, we encountered a highly influential field (attracting 100s of million USD of research funding in the US only over the last decade) where nearly all previous theoretical and experimental work was faulty; some of the errors were obvious and even infuriating. moreover, they were repeatedly done by some of the most famous scientists in my community and published in a decade-long series of papers in journals of the highest profile.  while the series of comments we wrote on these papers had some effect, our experience is that the most important ingredients were missing. those are   1. echoing of our criticism by leading researchers - while countless senior scientists in our community expressed full support in our criticism in private conversations, none had expressed it in public. some used the excuse of "I cannot run after every single error by others", "these scientists are too big to "fall", or simply hinted that they must maintain good relations with those influential researchers that published the faulty results.  2. cooperation from editors - while we had one good experience with one Science editor, our experience with other editors of that journal, as well as editors in other Nature group and ACS journals was that they want to avoid the scandal, or avoid a conflict with senior scientists who published faulty results. one even wrote that "our journal does not publish criticism over earlier work, but instead focuses on high end original research"... in other words, our feeling is that the editors are more committed to the financial success and reputation of their journal (not to mention their professional relations with those shady researchers) rather than to truth...  It is our impression that without these ingredients, the pace of self-correction in science is significantly slowed down, or even abolished altogether.
CODE-h1: Funding or financial considerations at stake

Question: Q3_notAuth_10_TEXT
Verbatim: In most cases the only time I "take action" is when reviewing a paper, I will try to point out any vague and unsupported conclusions and ways that the conclusions could be verified in my mind. In the current publishing environment, I am also mindful of the fact that authors are dependent on publications to be able to work (research universities largely treat the research portion as a source of money and the researchers as tenants) and the fact that the manuscript I am reviewing will end up in the literature somewhere no matter what due to the number of places where one can publish. With these in mind, I consider it my "job" (unpaid, and, ironically, usually to put research behind a paywall), to reason with the authors and ask for logical changes that should appeal to them as scientists. When asking for new experiments, I think it is important to make them within the means of the authors, doable on a reasonable timeline, and essential to a conclusion presented in the paper. Unless it affects the entire paper, I will suggest simply removing the conclusion I feel is untenable.   I will also point out issues with data to my students and colleagues when it is important to collaborations or interpretation of our results. I will also sometimes discuss it directly with the authors at a conference is I know them well enough for it to not be confrontational. The main problem is that in most fields including mine, there is not enough data in the manuscript to know for certain which errors are being made. In fact, with current analysis methods this is not likely possible without significant time and money put into the data collection, analysis, storage, and organization methods, but the resources for these types of undertakings are not available in most areas of science, which is, perhaps, the root of the problem.
CODE-h1: Funding or financial considerations at stake

Verbatim: Academic scientific research is in my view highly sensationalist. The rewards for publishing "exciting" results are stronger than the rewards for publishing "rigorous" results. The consequence is that many high-profile studies make extraordinary claims but lack extraordinary evidence.  Sometimes the sensationalist "exciting" results that lack persuasive evidence turn out to be correct: either the researchers were lucky or the PI had good intuition. But sometimes, they turn out to be incorrect. Eventually people have trouble reproducing or building on the results. The trouble is that this self-correcting process can take far too long when the PI of the exciting result is a leader in the field and/or has a huge ego and fights fiercely to quash dissent.  The latter has been the case in my field, which only started about ten years ago. The claims are extraordinary, but most of the pioneering experiments come from one highly celebrated PI whose experiments lack the necessary controls to be persuasive. The field is now about ten years old and trouble is brewing: some of those who have attempted to reproduce experiments have failed, and theorists have struggled to come up with a theory that accurately predicts the experimental results. Our group currently has a paper under review which highlights the problem of designing rigorous control experiments in this field. We conducted an experiment that eliminated the problems and found that the conclusions of one pioneering paper could probably be explained by a trivial effect. During the review process 3 out of 4 reviewers acknowledged the problems we identified, but the fourth reviewer did not, and had a strangely intimate knowledge of the pioneering experiment whose results we challenged. (Hmm!) We will see if the paper gets published, but if it does not it will almost certainly be because ego is slowing down the scientific process.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: There are many unintentional errors that creep into work, or not even errors but places where we over-rationalize our own work and excuse (or don't even notice) deficiencies in our work. I'm always very appreciative when reviewers point out such issues in my manuscripts and I have the chance to consider and correct them. I try to pay that forward in my own reviewing work, where I try to take a respectful but critical eye to the papers that cross my desk, and don't just give them the 1 or 2 hour quick glance.   However, I feel that today's science doesn't reward correctness or thoroughness. We are all pushed to publish as many papers as possible, as quickly as possible, and preferably as flashy as possible (so they will be highly cited and put into well-regarded journals). This pressure not only encourages us to ignore (or take the time to correct) weaknesses in our own work, but also puts the average faculty member under tremendous time pressure, such that other service duties fundamental to science (rigorous and meaningful peer review, for example) are given short shrift.   Mistakes, disagreements, corrections, retractions, etc., are all fundamental to science and should be taken as such. Instead, we have poorly aligned incentives, and often adversarial relationships with the pubpeers and retractionwatches of the world. It's difficult to know how academic society should respond to these concerns, but in general in the few times I've been motivated to speak up regarding concerns about scientific correctness, I try to give the benefit of the doubt (I have been proven wrong by the authors of a "mistaken" paper before!). I think more rigor and more humility on the sides of both authors and reviewers is needed to keep scientific literature on a path to correctness.

Verbatim: I think one of the problems is to define what an error actually is. Verbatim: I would first want to encourage you on the choice of this project. in research, we encountered a highly influential field (attracting 100s of million USD of research funding in the US only over the last decade) where nearly all previous theoretical and experimental work was faulty; some of the errors were obvious and even infuriating. moreover, they were repeatedly done by some of the most famous scientists in my community and published in a decade-long series of papers in journals of the highest profile.  while the series of comments we wrote on these papers had some effect, our experience is that the most important ingredients were missing. those are   1. echoing of our criticism by leading researchers - while countless senior scientists in our community expressed full support in our criticism in private conversations, none had expressed it in public. some used the excuse of "I cannot run after every single error by others", "these scientists are too big to "fall", or simply hinted that they must maintain good relations with those influential researchers that published the faulty results.  2. cooperation from editors - while we had one good experience with one Science editor, our experience with other editors of that journal, as well as editors in other Nature group and ACS journals was that they want to avoid the scandal, or avoid a conflict with senior scientists who published faulty results. one even wrote that "our journal does not publish criticism over earlier work, but instead focuses on high end original research"... in other words, our feeling is that the editors are more committed to the financial success and reputation of their journal (not to mention their professional relations with those shady researchers) rather than to truth... Question: Q9_Auth_14_TEXT
Verbatim: I don't know if "error" is the right word, but, as I said earlier, I consider the more subtle issues of interpretation of data and finding better ways to analyze data in the context of new discoveries to be more important than pointing out the few "cheaters" or stupid mistakes that appear on twitter occasionally for folks to laugh at and feel good about themselves. The instance I am thinking of is data I collected and interpreted in graduate school that I now suspect has a different meaning than I assigned it then. I, and the field, know much more and looking back it is likely a different case than was assigned in the paper. I am open about this and not trying to hide something (not that many people would care, I think - it isn't a huge field with dramatic consequences), but the current system makes errors like this very hard to address. I don't consider this "my" work since I was a PhD student, so it seems like an imposition to "refute" my former advisors work. Also, all new data would need to be collected and the synthesis is not something my lab does, and it would not be an "interesting" publication, so, if the conclusions to be drawn were, as I suspect, different than previously published, it would be an expensive, time-consuming, low-impact way to create some ill-will while ultimately changing very little.   The way to actually change this is in the way we are credited for data. If I had an annotated dataset in a searchable database that was separate from my analysis, then I could now reanalyze that data as part of a larger study that involved refining the model I had used (and also modifying the (currently non-existent) raw data check to test for the measurement errors that caused the analysis error in the first place). In this scenario, everyone wins because everyone with data that had the errors gets cited and they get their data reanalyzed in a new paper.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: To add a brief comment:  I rarely spotted an error that would completely bring down the paper. I often find that the results in a paper are interpreted in the wrong terms, but I would say this is quite common. We once published a paper to refute a particularly wrong paper, which sparked discussion at conferences and subsequent publications.   The majority of cases I am aware of consist of miscalculated experimental quantities that bring to an exaggerated claim. For example, is the effect 5% or 50%? The general feeling is that if the paper is not particularly impactful or brings incremental information in the field, the need for correction is ignored. Question: Q14
Verbatim: There are many unintentional errors that creep into work, or not even errors but places where we over-rationalize our own work and excuse (or don't even notice) deficiencies in our work. I'm always very appreciative when reviewers point out such issues in my manuscripts and I have the chance to consider and correct them. I try to pay that forward in my own reviewing work, where I try to take a respectful but critical eye to the papers that cross my desk, and don't just give them the 1 or 2 hour quick glance.   However, I feel that today's science doesn't reward correctness or thoroughness. We are all pushed to publish as many papers as possible, as quickly as possible, and preferably as flashy as possible (so they will be highly cited and put into well-regarded journals). This pressure not only encourages us to ignore (or take the time to correct) weaknesses in our own work, but also puts the average faculty member under tremendous time pressure, such that other service duties fundamental to science (rigorous and meaningful peer review, for example) are given short shrift.   Mistakes, disagreements, corrections, retractions, etc., are all fundamental to science and should be taken as such. Instead, we have poorly aligned incentives, and often adversarial relationships with the pubpeers and retractionwatches of the world. It's difficult to know how academic society should respond to these concerns, but in general in the few times I've been motivated to speak up regarding concerns about scientific correctness, I try to give the benefit of the doubt (I have been proven wrong by the authors of a "mistaken" paper before!). I think more rigor and more humility on the sides of both authors and reviewers is needed to keep scientific literature on a path to correctness.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Academic scientific research is in my view highly sensationalist. The rewards for publishing "exciting" results are stronger than the rewards for publishing "rigorous" results. The consequence is that many high-profile studies make extraordinary claims but lack extraordinary evidence.  Sometimes the sensationalist "exciting" results that lack persuasive evidence turn out to be correct: either the researchers were lucky or the PI had good intuition. But sometimes, they turn out to be incorrect. Eventually people have trouble reproducing or building on the results. The trouble is that this self-correcting process can take far too long when the PI of the exciting result is a leader in the field and/or has a huge ego and fights fiercely to quash dissent.  The latter has been the case in my field, which only started about ten years ago. The claims are extraordinary, but most of the pioneering experiments come from one highly celebrated PI whose experiments lack the necessary controls to be persuasive. The field is now about ten years old and trouble is brewing: some of those who have attempted to reproduce experiments have failed, and theorists have struggled to come up with a theory that accurately predicts the experimental results. Our group currently has a paper under review which highlights the problem of designing rigorous control experiments in this field. This pressure not only encourages us to ignore (or take the time to correct) weaknesses in our own work, but also puts the average faculty member under tremendous time pressure, such that other service duties fundamental to science (rigorous and meaningful peer review, for example) are given short shrift.   Mistakes, disagreements, corrections, retractions, etc., are all fundamental to science and should be taken as such. Instead, we have poorly aligned incentives, and often adversarial relationships with the pubpeers and retractionwatches of the world. It's difficult to know how academic society should respond to these concerns, but in general in the few times I've been motivated to speak up regarding concerns about scientific correctness, I try to give the benefit of the doubt (I have been proven wrong by the authors of a "mistaken" paper before!). Question: Q9_Auth_14_TEXT
Verbatim: I don't know if "error" is the right word, but, as I said earlier, I consider the more subtle issues of interpretation of data and finding better ways to analyze data in the context of new discoveries to be more important than pointing out the few "cheaters" or stupid mistakes that appear on twitter occasionally for folks to laugh at and feel good about themselves. The instance I am thinking of is data I collected and interpreted in graduate school that I now suspect has a different meaning than I assigned it then. I, and the field, know much more and looking back it is likely a different case than was assigned in the paper. I am open about this and not trying to hide something (not that many people would care, I think - it isn't a huge field with dramatic consequences), but the current system makes errors like this very hard to address. I don't consider this "my" work since I was a PhD student, so it seems like an imposition to "refute" my former advisors work. Also, all new data would need to be collected and the synthesis is not something my lab does, and it would not be an "interesting" publication, so, if the conclusions to be drawn were, as I suspect, different than previously published, it would be an expensive, time-consuming, low-impact way to create some ill-will while ultimately changing very little.   The way to actually change this is in the way we are credited for data. If I had an annotated dataset in a searchable database that was separate from my analysis, then I could now reanalyze that data as part of a larger study that involved refining the model I had used (and also modifying the (currently non-existent) raw data check to test for the measurement errors that caused the analysis error in the first place). In this scenario, everyone wins because everyone with data that had the errors gets cited and they get their data reanalyzed in a new paper.
CODE-h1: Time consuming to correct, lack of time

Verbatim: In all cases that I can recall, the errors in manuscripts that I have identified been have been corrected by the authors before publication. Question: Q14
Verbatim: In my field, synthetic chemistry, there are thousands of papers that seemingly exaggerate yields of reactions. When we repeat, we frequently do not get close to reported yields.   In lesser journals there are results reported that are clearly fabricated, but no one has the time or energy to call out these lies. I believe this happens very frequently.

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Oftentimes errors in scientific manuscripts fail to be identified in peer review, because many reviewers don't dedicate the necessary time to read and check the manuscript carefully. I remember the case of a manuscript for a review article that I reviewed, which was >150 manuscript pages and had dozens of factual errors in it (e.g., contributions to a research field being attributed to the wrong group, data in primary sources being misinterpreted, results being discussed based on outdated models), yet the second reviewer recommended publication as is, probably because they had never read it in full. More recently, I reviewed a research article in which some quantitative data were off by a factor of 1000 (µM instead of mM), yet I was the only one of four reviewers to notice the mistake. On several occasions, I have found errors in published works of other researchers, and usually I contact these authors to inform them about the mistake. Some have immediately decided to publish a Corrigendum, others have not published a Corrigendum but have mentioned the error in a follow-up publication on the same topic, and yet others have taken no action at all.
CODE-h1: Time consuming to correct, lack of time

Question: Q14
Verbatim: Manuscript editing at journals is getting very lax.  After receiving a galley proof for a paper I published at [ANONYMISED]., I sent back a rather long list of corrections. I then wrote a whole manuscript (which was published in [ANONYMISED]) debunking his theory.  He is still publishing, and was recently selected to give the "[ANONYMISED]" featured lecture at the [ANONYMISED] Summit this month in [ANONYMISED].  If peer reviewers took their job seriously, they would have seen the fallacies in his arguments, interpretations, and conclusions, and never published these papers.
